reasoning: The base rate of the US holding an election that resolves as "free and fair" under these specific, high-bar criteria is historically near 100%. Major domestic and international observer organizations (like the OSCE and the Carter Center) have never concluded that a U.S. election fundamentally failed to meet basic democratic standards, even amid the polarization of 2020 and 2024. Furthermore, widespread, outcome-determinative invalidation of results across multiple states by courts is highly improbable due to the decentralized nature of US elections and strict legal precedents requiring an extraordinarily high burden of proof. While the April 2026 political environment features aggressive maneuvers by the federal executive—specifically the DOJ's controversial attempts to compile a national voter database—which has sparked legitimate concern and widespread state-level lawsuits, judicial and institutional checks remain formidable. These disputes are highly likely to remain localized or be resolved without triggering the strict "widespread" and "outcome-determinative" benchmarks outlined in the resolution criteria. Aligning with the recent community consensus of 85%, I maintain a very high probability, grading down slightly to reflect the artificial multi-date requirement.
